title: Chicken-fried steak
subtitle: Battered cube steak with cream gravy.
excerpt: Texan diner classic: a thin piece of beef pounded out, dredged in seasoned flour, dipped in buttermilk, dredged again, and pan-fried until the coating shatters. Cream gravy made in the same pan from the brown bits. Two pieces of steak per plate, mash on the side.
sourceNotes: Texas Hill Country chuck-wagon tradition, codified through the WPA state surveys of the 1930s. Cream-gravy technique follows the Southern country-gravy canon documented in early-twentieth-century farmhouse manuals.

content.text: Cheap cuts of beef hammered out flat so they cook fast. Coated in flour, dipped in buttermilk, coated again. Pan-fried in oil hot enough that the coating crackles on contact. The pan goes from beef to gravy in one pour: drained oil out, fresh flour in to soak up the brown bits, then milk whisked in until silky. Salt heavily, pepper heavily, pour over the steak and the mash.
Two pieces per person, served on a wide plate next to mashed potato and either green beans or corn. The gravy is the whole point: don't be tempted to make it elsewhere.

items.name: Beef chuck
items.prepNote: thin-cut top round or chuck, 4 pieces ~ 200 g each

content.text: A wide 30 cm cast-iron or heavy frying pan that takes two steaks at a time. A meat mallet for pounding the steaks; the smooth side, not the toothed one, for this cut.

Lay each piece of beef between two sheets of cling film. Hammer with the smooth side of the mallet until the steak is around 6 mm thick and roughly the size of your hand. Don't tear the meat; even, firm strokes.

Whisk {{plain-flour}} of flour with {{paprika-sweet}} of paprika, {{cayenne}} of cayenne, {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt, and {{black-pepper}} of pepper in a shallow bowl. In a second bowl, whisk {{buttermilk}} of buttermilk with {{eggs}} of beaten egg. Dredge each steak in flour, then in the buttermilk mixture, then back in flour, pressing the coating on firmly. Place on a tray. Let stand 15 minutes; this dries the coating so it adheres in the pan.

Pour {{vegetable-oil}} of oil into the pan so it sits 1 cm deep. Heat to 180°C, or until a pinch of flour sizzles fiercely on contact. Lay in two steaks; don't crowd. Fry for 3 minutes a side until deep golden and crisp. Lift onto a wire rack over a tray and keep warm in a 100°C oven while the rest cook.

Pour off all but 4 tablespoons of oil from the pan, leaving the brown bits at the bottom. Set over medium heat. Sprinkle in {{plain-flour}} of flour and whisk for 1 minute to a smooth pale paste. Slowly pour in {{whole-milk}} of milk, whisking constantly, until you have a smooth gravy. Cook for 5 minutes until thickened. Season hard with {{black-pepper}} of pepper and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt; this gravy needs more pepper than feels reasonable.
Plate one or two steaks per person. Ladle a generous spoonful of gravy over the top. Mash and green beans on the side. Eat at once.

items.fix: Rest the dredged steaks on a tray for 15 minutes before frying. Don't move the steak around once it goes in; let the coating set.
items.cause: Dredge not given time to dry, or pan too crowded.
items.symptom: Coating slid off in the pan

items.fix: Pound to 6 mm, the thickness of a coin. The mechanical tenderising is what makes the cheap cut workable.
Pour the milk slowly while whisking. If lumps form, push through a fine sieve back into the pan.
items.cause: Milk poured in too fast, or roux not whisked smooth.

items.fix: Simmer for the full 5 minutes; the starch needs heat to thicken. If still thin, whisk in another tablespoon of flour mixed with cold water.
Always heat the oil to 180°C before the first steak goes in. A drop of buttermilk should sizzle and bubble on contact.
items.cause: Oil not hot enough at the start.

content.text: Chicken-fried steak grew out of the cowboy kitchens of nineteenth-century Texas, where tough cuts of cattle were pounded thin and fried like a piece of chicken. It became the breakfast-and-dinner staple of every Texas diner from Lamesa to Lubbock. Outside the state, the same plate goes by country-fried steak and tends to come with a brown gravy instead of the white cream version.
